what.note: Declarative framework for batch and streaming ETL pipelines.

fact.note: Introduced at the 2021 Data + AI Summit, it let engineers build entire production pipelines by sprinkling the keyword LIVE into ordinary SQL and letting the framework untangle the dependencies for them.

limitations.note: A workspace is limited to 1000 concurrent pipeline updates; a pipeline allows at most 100 source files (or 50 folder entries referencing up to 1000 files); pivot() is unsupported, Delta time travel works only on streaming tables not materialized views, and identity columns are incompatible with AUTO CDC processing.

what.note: Declarative framework for batch and streaming ETL pipelines.

fact.note: You describe the tables you want and it works out the execution order and dependencies for you - and the DLT code written back in 2021 still runs unchanged under the new name.
The open-source engine underneath is a separate project called Spark Declarative Pipelines, and Databricks docs increasingly shorten the product to just 'Lakeflow pipelines.'

what.note: Orchestration for multi-task jobs and pipelines.

fact.note: Databricks' answer to 'do we really need Airflow for this?', it wired tasks into DAGs right inside the platform and added a Repair and Rerun button so you could retry only the tasks that flopped.

limitations.note: A workspace is limited to 2000 concurrent task runs, 10000 job creations per hour, 12000 saved jobs, and 1000 tasks per job; job parameters using dynamic values are capped at 10000 characters.

what.note: Orchestration for multi-task jobs and pipelines.

fact.note: A single job can chain notebooks, SQL, Python, dbt, and even other jobs into one dependency graph - Databricks' original orchestrator, now reunited with pipelines under the Lakeflow umbrella.
In the console it now lives under a combined 'Jobs & Pipelines' section of the sidebar.

links.label: Databricks Blog: What's New Lakeflow Jobs

what.note: SQL-optimized compute that powers Databricks SQL.

fact.note: It was the elastic compute you pointed your BI tool at, spinning up to run SQL queries - Databricks even creates a small 'Starter Warehouse' for you automatically.

limitations.note: The maximum number of queries in a warehouse queue is 1000 for all warehouse types, and pro/classic SKUs have a fixed limit of one cluster per 10 concurrent queries.

what.note: SQL-optimized compute that powers Databricks SQL.

fact.note: The serverless flavor spins up in seconds - typically 2 to 6 - where a classic warehouse takes several minutes (around four) to start.
Sources can't agree on when the rename happened: Databricks docs date it to 2023, while some write-ups swear it was 2022.

what.note: In-workspace Git client for version-controlling notebooks and files.

fact.note: Born to fix data teams' 'poor or no integration with Git,' it turned each notebook into a versioned file you could branch, review and commit without ever leaving the workspace.

limitations.note: Working branches are capped at 1 GB, files over 10 MB can't be viewed in the UI, each Git operation supports up to 2 GB memory and 4 GB disk, and Databricks recommends under 20000 files; GPG commit signing and SSH aren't supported, and Git operations that alter notebook source lose cell outputs and state.

what.note: In-workspace Git client for version-controlling notebooks and files.

fact.note: It's a full Git client living inside the workspace - branch, commit, pull - and it was renamed 'Git folders' once it grew to version arbitrary files, not just notebooks.
It can version arbitrary file types, not just notebooks, but it refuses to work without a remote repo URL.
The switch to the 'Git folders' name landed in the March 21, 2024 platform release notes.

links.label: Flexera: Set Up Databricks Git Folders

what.note: In-workspace UI for browsing Unity Catalog data - schemas and tables - viewing sample data, and handing out grants.

fact.note: The point-and-click window into Unity Catalog: browse schemas, peek at sample data and hand out grants without writing a line of SQL.

limitations.note: The Catalog Explorer UI is not available in all Databricks workspaces.

what.note: In-workspace UI for browsing and managing Unity Catalog securables - schemas, tables, models, functions, and other assets.

fact.note: It got promoted from 'Data Explorer' once it started managing models, functions, and volumes too - not just tables - though the sidebar still just calls it 'Catalog.'
Despite the promotion it still shows up as plain 'Catalog' in the sidebar, and it now governs every Unity Catalog securable, not just tables.

links.label: Tabular Editor: Explore with Catalog Explorer

what.note: AI-assisted dashboards for visualizing and sharing query results.

fact.note: Entered public preview on September 28, 2023 (AWS and Azure first, GCP in the first half of 2024), pairing a new visualization engine that rendered charts up to 10x faster with draft/publish editing and Unity Catalog lineage.
It launched as 'Lakeview dashboards,' and the REST API still answers to that name - endpoints live under /api/2.0/lakeview - long after the marketing stopped using it.

limitations.note: A dashboard supports up to 15 pages, 100 datasets, and 100 widgets per page; most charts render at most 10000 rows (tables up to 100K before truncation), and email-subscription attachments are capped at 9 MB combined with 100000 rows per Excel attachment.

what.note: AI-assisted dashboards for visualizing and sharing query results.

fact.note: You can build most chart types just by describing them to Databricks Assistant in plain English on the dashboard canvas - though it sees only table and column metadata, not row values, so data-specific requests can trip it up.

what.note: Infrastructure-as-code format for defining and deploying Databricks resources (jobs, pipelines, etc.) as a versioned bundle.

fact.note: Databricks' take on infrastructure-as-code: a single databricks.yml that packaged jobs, pipelines and notebooks into one versioned unit you could test and ship from dev to prod.

links.label: Data Eng Central: CI/CD with DABs

what.note: Infrastructure-as-code format for defining and deploying Databricks resources (jobs, pipelines, etc.) as a versioned bundle.

fact.note: Describe an entire project's Databricks resources - jobs, pipelines, dashboards, model serving endpoints - as YAML source files, then validate, deploy, and run the whole bundle from the Databricks CLI.
The 2026 rename kept the affectionate 'DABs' acronym, so the docs now read 'Declarative Automation Bundles (formerly Databricks Asset Bundles).'

links.label: Mechanical Rock: An Ode to DABs

what.note: Curated, governed conversational query spaces scoped to a business domain, letting business users ask questions of their structured data in plain English (text-to-SQL).

fact.note: A Genie Space was a topic-scoped room where analysts hand-fed the AI tables, metric definitions and sample queries, so a business user could ask 'how's my sales pipeline?' and get an answer instead of Slacking the data team.

what.note: Governed, curated conversational agents that give business users a natural-language chat interface to ask questions of their data in Databricks.

fact.note: There's real compute behind the chat box: every Genie Agent runs on a pro or serverless SQL warehouse and can only reach data you've registered in Unity Catalog.

limitations.note: Up to 30 tables or views per Genie Agent; up to 10,000 conversations, each with up to 10,000 messages; requires a pro or serverless SQL warehouse, with data registered in Unity Catalog.

links.label: Databricks Blog: Genie Spaces Best Practices

what.note: Business-user-facing UI giving non-technical users a single conversational entry point to data and AI.

fact.note: A deliberately code-free front door for executives and marketers who never touch a notebook, bundling dashboards, natural-language questions and custom apps behind one un-technical home screen, with no seat-based licenses required.

what.note: Business-user-facing UI giving non-technical users a single conversational entry point to data and AI.

fact.note: For a few weeks in the spring of 2026 the simplified business UI was simply called 'Genie' - Databricks reusing the name of its conversational-analytics feature for the whole front door - before June's rename tacked on 'One' to disambiguate.
For a few weeks the AI/BI release notes literally said the simplified interface 'has been renamed to Genie. The feature and its capabilities are unchanged.'

occasion.note: Renamed from Databricks One to Genie, April 27, 2026

limitations.note: Workspaces using the compliance security profile are excluded from account-level Genie One aggregation, and account-level Genie One may process Databricks-generated metadata in the US, so it should be disabled if compliance policies prohibit US metadata transmission.

what.note: Business-user-facing UI giving non-technical users a single conversational entry point to data and AI.

fact.note: A deliberately no-notebooks front door for business users - executives and marketers who never open a notebook - that launched as Databricks One, spent a few weeks as plain 'Genie', and settled on Genie One in June 2026.
On top of the original no-notebooks experience it went mobile: Databricks launched native iOS and Android apps that, in its words, 'put Genie in your pocket.'

occasion.note: Renamed from Genie to Genie One, June 9, 2026

what.note: Managed vector (similarity) search over Unity Catalog data, turning a Delta table into an auto-syncing vector index for RAG apps.

fact.note: Launched to give RAG apps a serverless similarity search that could turn any Delta table into an auto-syncing vector index with one click, all governed by the same Unity Catalog permissions as the underlying data.

what.note: Managed, serverless vector (similarity) search over Unity Catalog data, with one-click Delta-to-index sync, scaling to billions of embeddings.

fact.note: Reached general availability as a serverless vector database boasting one-click Delta-to-index sync and, per its makers, up to 5x faster performance than rival vector stores while scaling to billions of embeddings.

links.label: DB blog: Mosaic AI Vector Search GA
Microsoft: Mosaic AI Vector Search GA

limitations.note: Row- and column-level permissions aren't supported, indexes can't be cloned across workspaces, and the _id column name is reserved. Query results are capped (10000 rows for ANN, 200 for hybrid/full-text) with a 10 MB max response, and storage-optimized endpoints add limits such as no continuous sync, embedding dimensions divisible by 16, and no CMK or FedRAMP support.

what.note: Managed search over Unity Catalog data; now supports full-text and hybrid search without pre-computed embeddings.

fact.note: It outgrew pure vector search - it now also does full-text keyword search and hybrid keyword-similarity search, and on storage-optimized endpoints you can even build an index with no precomputed embedding columns at all.

links.label: Matillion: Use Mosaic AI Vector Search

what.note: The SQL query, dashboard, and BI service that let data analysts run data-warehouse-style workloads directly on the data lake.

fact.note: Debuted in November 2020 to let analysts run plain BI and reporting workloads directly on the data lake, with a SQL-native query and visualization interface and native connectors for major BI tools like Tableau and Power BI: warehouse habits at lake prices.

limitations.note: Databricks limits queries on a SQL warehouse by compute cost; classic and pro warehouses have a fixed limit of one cluster per 10 concurrent queries, and autoscaling only adds clusters up to the configured maximum.

what.note: The serverless data warehouse for running SQL queries, dashboards, and BI on the lakehouse.

fact.note: Easy to confuse with SQL Warehouse, but one's the product and the other's the compute engine that runs it - and it dropped the name 'SQL Analytics' back in 2021.
When it was renamed in May 2021, even the access entitlement changed: 'SQL Analytics access' became 'Databricks SQL access.'

what.note: The proprietary Databricks table format that adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel to data lakes.

fact.note: Unveiled at Spark Summit Europe 2017 as a single system fusing a data lake's scale, a warehouse's query speed and a streaming pipe's low latency, storing everything as plain Parquet on S3 while promising up to 100x faster queries.

what.note: The open table format that adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel to data lakes.

fact.note: It lets you 'time travel' - query a table exactly as it looked versions ago - and the format went from proprietary 'Databricks Delta' to open source in April 2019.
Later in 2019 the format was donated to the Linux Foundation, which now hosts it under an open-governance model as an open standard for data lakes.

links.label: dev.to: Delta Lake ACID & time travel

what.note: Agent Bricks capability that orchestrates multiple specialized agents, routing each request to the right one.

fact.note: A control-plane conductor for enterprise AI: it reads your intent, chops a messy question into subtasks, routes each to the best specialist agent, and stitches the replies back into a single cited answer.

limitations.note: A single supervisor system can't use more than 50 agents, AI Search index subagents support only Delta Sync indexes, and workspaces with Enhanced Security and Compliance aren't supported. Web search requires databricks-gpt-5 in the system.ai allowlist and is unavailable in HIPAA/BAA workspaces or regions lacking web-search-capable models.

what.note: Agent Bricks capability that orchestrates multiple specialized agents, routing each request to the right one.

fact.note: It's an agent whose entire job is bossing other agents around - routing each request to the right specialist - and its doc URL still reads /multi-agent-supervisor.

what.note: Databricks' automated, declarative way to build production AI agents: you describe the task in natural language and connect your data, and it auto-generates evaluations and auto-optimizes the agent.

fact.note: Instead of hand-tuning prompts, it automatically builds evaluation benchmarks specific to your task - sometimes synthetically generating new data or custom LLM judges - then searches across optimization techniques like prompt engineering and fine-tuning to auto-optimize the agent.

There was never a single 'Agent Bricks GA' day - individual capabilities reach general availability on their own schedules (Document Intelligence, for instance, went GA at Data + AI Summit 2026), by which point Databricks says 100k+ agents had been built on it, together processing over a quadrillion tokens a year.

links.label: Databricks blog: Agent Bricks at DAIS 2026

limitations.note: Information Extraction agents have a 128k-token maximum context length, and union schema types aren't supported.

what.note: Agent Bricks capability with a no-code UI that turns unstructured documents into a structured table by extracting fields against a schema.

fact.note: You describe what you want in plain English and click Generate Schema - it writes the extraction schema for you; under the hood it runs on the ai_extract SQL function.
The original launch capability now sits at a '(legacy)' docs page, replaced by a rebuild on the ai_extract v2 signature.

occasion.note: Data + AI Summit, June 2025 (beta); rebuilt on the ai_extract v2 signature, Public Preview March 2026.

links.label: Azure Databricks release notes: March 2026 (Public Preview)

limitations.note: Files larger than 100 MB and documents over 500 pages (except text and markdown) are skipped during ingestion, as are files whose names start with an underscore or period. For table sources only the selected content column is processed, and AI Search indexes must use a supported embedding model (databricks-gte-large-en, databricks-bge-large-en, or databricks-qwen3-embedding-0-6b).

what.note: Agent Bricks capability: a managed, document-grounded Q&A chatbot that answers questions over your uploaded documents with citations.

fact.note: Rather than plain similarity search it uses an "Instructed Retriever" that learns how each knowledge source is organized - Databricks claims up to 70% higher answer quality than simplistic RAG, with page-level citations on every response.
Don't confuse it with 'Knowledge Assurance' - that isn't an official Databricks name.

occasion.note: Data + AI Summit, June 2025 (beta); GA January 2026.

limitations.note: Built on ai_classify, which isn't available on Databricks SQL Classic and can't be used with views. Labels must number between 2 and 500 unique values, with names of 1-100 characters and descriptions of 0-1000 characters, and total context is capped at 128000 tokens.

what.note: Agent Bricks capability with a no-code UI that sorts documents and unstructured text into categories you define, built on the ai_classify SQL function.

fact.note: The builder previews on just five rows at a time - it "classifies up to 5 documents and rows and displays the results" - so you tune labels on a tiny sample with thumbs-up/down feedback, then scale out via SQL or a Lakeflow pipeline.
It leans on the ai_classify SQL function, whose v2 signature reached GA in June 2026.

occasion.note: Public Preview March 2026; ai_classify GA at Data + AI Summit June 2026.

links.label: Azure Databricks release notes: March 2026 (Public Preview)

what.note: Agent Bricks capability for building a task-specialized text LLM from your own data - summarization, classification, transformation, and generation - with auto-optimized cost versus quality.

fact.note: On "Optimize" it races several strategies (including Foundation Model fine-tuning) and can deploy a cheaper model that matches the quality of a bigger one; the guidelines you accept double as auto-generated pass/fail evaluation metrics.
It shipped as Beta at the June 2025 summit and never reached GA - its docs page now carries a '(legacy)' title while still flagged Beta.

what.note: AI coding assistant that generates and fixes SQL and Python across notebooks, the SQL editor, and pipelines, grounded in Unity Catalog metadata.

fact.note: A context-aware helper wired into notebooks, the SQL editor and file editor that turns plain-English requests into SQL or Python, explains gnarly code, and quietly reads your Unity Catalog metadata to tailor answers.

limitations.note: Databricks recommends always reviewing generated code before running it, since it can make mistakes, and warns that auto-approve is a best-effort productivity heuristic, not a security boundary.

what.note: AI coding assistant that generates and fixes SQL and Python across notebooks, the SQL editor, and pipelines, grounded in Unity Catalog metadata.

fact.note: Unlike a generic code assistant, it reads your Unity Catalog metadata - so its SQL and Python suggestions already know your real table and column names.
Databricks' launch blog framed it as the newest member of the Genie family rather than a straight rename.

what.note: Databricks Labs CLI that templated and deployed jobs via the Jobs API.

fact.note: A Databricks Labs side project - the name is short for 'Databricks CLI eXtensions' - that templated and deployed jobs to the Jobs API straight from your terminal.
Its config lived in a .dbx/project.json file, a different format entirely, which is why moving off it is a migration rather than a flip of a switch.

links.label: Medium: Pipelines in Databricks with dbx

what.note: Databricks SQL's original built-in dashboards for pinning query visualizations onto a shared, drag-and-drop canvas.

fact.note: Databricks' dashboards trace back to Redash, the popular open-source visualization and dashboarding tool it acquired in June 2020.
They shipped as part of SQL Analytics, which Databricks unveiled in November 2020, letting analysts build shareable dashboards straight from their query visualizations.

what.note: The deprecation-era name for the original Redash-based Databricks SQL dashboards.

fact.note: Databricks switched these off for good - any that were not converted first were permanently deleted on March 2, 2026.
Databricks had already stopped letting anyone create or clone them through the UI or API back on April 10, 2025.

links.label: Databricks docs: Clone a legacy dashboard to AI/BI

what.note: The original Python-based command-line interface (versions 0.18 and below).

fact.note: It was a Python package (versions 0.18 and below) that carried an official 'Experimental' label for its entire life and never graduated out of it.
Databricks says it 'has not established a date or timeline for deprecating the legacy CLI' - it is simply unsupported and frozen.

what.note: Mounting cloud object storage into the DBFS namespace under /mnt.

fact.note: A single `dbutils.fs.mount` made a cloud storage bucket look like a local /mnt path to everyone on the cluster - convenient, and entirely outside per-user governance.
New workspaces are now provisioned without mount access at all.

links.label: Community: DBFS mounts no longer recommended

what.note: Highly available, low-latency service for deploying ML and GenAI models behind autoscaling REST endpoints.

fact.note: Puts a trained model behind a REST endpoint and manages the servers for you, elastically scaling capacity up under load and back down when the traffic dries up.

limitations.note: Documented limits include up to 1000 endpoints per workspace (raisable on request), a 16 MB request payload (4 MB for AI agent endpoints), a 597-second model execution duration per request, 50 create/update operations per 5-minute window, provisioned concurrency of up to 1024 per model and 4096 per workspace (raisable on request), a queries-per-second cap of 300,000 with route optimization (200 without, recommended only for small dev use), and no logging for requests or responses over 1 MB.

what.note: Highly available, low-latency service for deploying ML and GenAI models behind autoscaling REST endpoints.

fact.note: It shed the tongue-twister name 'Serverless Real-Time Inference' when it reached general availability in March 2023.
Some surfaces later slapped a 'Mosaic AI' prefix on it.

what.note: The legacy per-workspace MLflow model registry, including model stages (None/Staging/Production/Archived).

fact.note: It tracked each model through named stages - None, Staging, Production, Archived - and lived inside a single workspace, one isolated registry per workspace.
Since April 2024 it is switched off by default for new Unity Catalog accounts, though MLflow 3 still supports it.

links.label: Marvelous MLOps: migrating off Workspace Registry

what.note: The legacy per-workspace feature store, for workspaces not enabled for Unity Catalog, with its own Feature Store UI to browse and search features.

fact.note: It shipped as the databricks-feature-store Python package, whose FeatureStoreClient API created and managed feature tables; that package is now deprecated, its modules folded into databricks-feature-engineering.
It is available only in workspaces created before August 19, 2024.

limitations.note: Clustering-on-write doesn't support source queries with filters, joins, or aggregations (DBR 15.1 and below); you can't create a liquid-clustered table via Structured Streaming write (DBR 15.4 LTS and below); and automatic liquid clustering and row-level concurrency aren't available for managed Apache Iceberg v2 tables.

what.note: Delta Lake data-layout optimization that supersedes Z-ordering and Hive-style partitioning; clustering keys can evolve without rewriting existing data.

fact.note: It replaces both partitioning and Z-ordering, and unlike either you can change the clustering keys later without rewriting a single existing file - old ZORDER columns can even be reused as-is.
Databricks now recommends it for all new tables, over both partitioning and Z-ordering.

occasion.note: GA on Databricks Runtime 15.4 LTS and above

limitations.note: Volumes require Databricks Runtime 13.3 LTS or above or a SQL warehouse, don't support dbutils.fs commands distributed to executors, can't be accessed from RDDs or Unity Catalog UDFs, can't back a custom Hadoop file system, and require fully qualified /Volumes paths (no abbreviated paths).

what.note: Unity Catalog objects that govern non-tabular data (files of any format) alongside tables - a recommended alternative to the deprecated DBFS mounts.

fact.note: Volumes bring PDFs, images, and any other non-tabular files under the same Unity Catalog governance as your tables - a folder of files and a table, governed side by side.
They come in two flavors: managed volumes on Databricks-managed storage, and external volumes over your own cloud storage.

limitations.note: Federated queries are read-only (except legacy Hive metastore federation), don't use Databricks query caching (Result Cache and Disk Cache), lowercase table and schema names, ignore names invalid in Unity Catalog, and can't federate foreign tables with case-sensitive identifiers for Azure Synapse or Redshift.

what.note: Query external databases (Postgres, MySQL, Snowflake, Redshift, and more) in place through Unity Catalog foreign catalogs, with query pushdown and governance - no data movement.

fact.note: Query Postgres, Snowflake, or Redshift right where they live through 'foreign catalogs' - with pushdown, so the filtering happens on the source system and the data never moves.
The access is strictly read-only - you can query the foreign system but never write back to it.

what.note: The ODBC driver for connecting BI tools and applications to Databricks SQL warehouses and clusters.

fact.note: An OEM driver licensed from Simba Technologies that let Tableau and other BI tools speak ODBC to your cluster; its lineage traces back to a 2014 driver built for Shark, Spark's early SQL front-end.

limitations.note: Cloud Fetch applies only to query results larger than 1 MB, requires an E2 workspace and an S3 bucket without versioning, and needs ODBC driver 2.6.17 or above; with bucket versioning enabled, Databricks can't garbage-collect older result files after the 24-hour retention, which can grow storage.

what.note: The ODBC driver for connecting BI tools and applications to Databricks SQL warehouses and clusters.

fact.note: For years this was the third-party 'Simba Spark' driver in disguise - Databricks finally put its own name on it in 2026, and existing DSNs never noticed the change.
Databricks stopped shipping new Simba-branded versions but promised to support existing ones for two more years.

what.note: Open protocol and Databricks service for securely sharing live data across organizations and platforms, with or without Databricks.

fact.note: Launched as an open protocol that enabled secure data sharing across products for the first time, it lets a recipient pull Delta Lake or Parquet data straight into pandas or Tableau without first adopting the provider's platform.

limitations.note: Shared tabular data must be Delta or managed Iceberg; providers can't share tables using liquid clustering with partition filtering, collations, row filters, column masks, or SHALLOW CLONE; notebooks, volumes, and AI models can only be shared Databricks-to-Databricks; and shareable views must be defined on Delta tables or other shareable views.

what.note: Open protocol and Databricks service for securely sharing data and AI assets across organizations and platforms, with or without Databricks.

fact.note: It's an open protocol, so you can share a live table with someone who's never touched Databricks - and in 2026 the standard itself was open-sourced as OpenSharing at opensharing.io.
Its scope grew beyond data sharing to cover the full AI stack, with the standard handed to the Linux Foundation.

what.note: The IDE for developing Lakeflow (Spark Declarative) pipelines - code-first, folder-based, with selective execution, data previews, and pipeline graphs on one surface.

fact.note: A folder-based IDE that breaks pipeline logic out of single notebooks into modular Python and SQL files, complete with tabs, a dependency graph, per-table run buttons, and a cross-file issues panel.

what.note: The IDE for developing Lakeflow (Spark Declarative) pipelines - code-first, folder-based, with selective execution, data previews, and pipeline graphs on one surface.

fact.note: A code-first IDE that shows your pipeline graph, data previews, and selective runs on one screen - though its URL still betrays the old name, 'multi-file editor.'
It became generally available in May 2026, bringing an agent-first Genie Code experience for creating and debugging production pipelines.

what.note: The per-workspace metastore that catalogs tables and views within a single workspace, predating Unity Catalog.

fact.note: It scopes every table and view to a single workspace, and those tables still appear under a top-level catalog called hive_metastore.
Databricks accounts created after December 18, 2025 have no access to it, alongside DBFS root and mounts and no-isolation shared compute, all off by default.

links.label: Medium: Migrating Hive Metastore to UC

limitations.note: Lakebase Postgres Autoscaling enforces per-project limits: a default of 20 concurrently active computes (the default branch is exempt), up to 500 branches, 500 Postgres roles, and 500 databases per branch, a 16 TB storage quota per branch, and up to 1000 projects per workspace.

what.note: A fully managed, serverless Postgres (OLTP) database integrated into the Databricks platform, for building real-time transactional apps alongside lakehouse data.

fact.note: It's a managed serverless Postgres for transactional apps - with instant database branching, so you can fork a whole database's worth of state in seconds.
In August 2025 the Compute section's 'OLTP Database' tab was quietly renamed 'Lakebase Postgres.'

limitations.note: Supports read (SELECT) queries only - no writes or ETL - and doesn't support Genie, Genie Agents, or Jobs tasks. It excludes many table types (system, Delta Sharing, UC default-storage/external, Hive metastore, foreign/federated), the ADBC/ODBC/Go drivers, GEOGRAPHY/GEOMETRY types, AI functions, Python UDFs, and the compliance security profile.

what.note: A serverless compute engine for low-latency, high-concurrency analytical queries, delivering sub-second SQL reads directly on Unity Catalog Delta and Iceberg tables.

fact.note: It delivers sub-second queries straight off Delta and Iceberg tables - no separate serving database required - powered by a brand-new engine codenamed 'Reyden.'
It is in Beta for select read-only workloads - you contact your account team to switch it on.

limitations.note: By default, operators process only a limited sample of data; setting Rows scanned to Max reruns all upstream operators over the complete, unbounded dataset and can take a long time.

what.note: A visual, no-code, AI-native experience for building ETL pipelines with a drag-and-drop canvas and natural language, backed by production-ready Lakeflow pipeline code.

fact.note: Drag boxes on a canvas or describe the flow in plain English, and it emits real, production-ready pipeline code - so the prototype and the production job end up being the same thing.
It went from Public Preview in April 2026 to GA just two months later, in June.

limitations.note: Requires serverless compute or Databricks Runtime 16.4+ and uses governed tags; policies can't be applied directly to views; quotas apply (e.g. 10000 policies per metastore, 50 per table, 20 principals per policy); time travel and cloning fail on policy-protected tables unless the principal is exempted; and there's no ABAC information schema (use the REST API).

what.note: Unity Catalog access-control model that grants or restricts access by evaluating governed tags (attributes) on securables, so one policy on a catalog or schema applies to everything beneath it.

fact.note: Tag a column 'pii' once and a single catalog-level policy masks it everywhere beneath - no more re-granting access table by table.
The same tag-driven policies do both row filtering and column masking, and tag-based GRANT policies arrived in Beta in June 2026.

links.label: Databricks Blog: ABAC GA in Unity Catalog

limitations.note: Path-based access isn't supported except in Compatibility Mode (which bypasses Unity Catalog access controls), managed Iceberg tables support only deep cloning (not shallow clone), and transactions that write to managed Iceberg tables are in Private Preview.

what.note: Apache Iceberg tables created and fully managed by Unity Catalog, readable and writable by external Iceberg engines through the Unity Catalog Iceberg REST Catalog API.

fact.note: These Iceberg tables are managed by Unity Catalog yet fully read/write to outside engines like Trino and Flink - through a REST catalog that even hands out temporary storage credentials.
The managed Iceberg tables still get Databricks perks like liquid clustering, predictive optimization, and materialized views.

limitations.note: A serverless workspace must be in a region that supports serverless compute, and because serverless compute and default storage aren't deployed in your own cloud account, cloud-provider credits or discounts don't apply to that usage.

what.note: A workspace preconfigured with serverless compute and default storage - a fully managed SaaS experience with no compute or storage running in your own cloud account.

fact.note: Nothing runs in your cloud account - compute and storage live entirely on Databricks' side, making it the closest thing to a pure-SaaS Databricks (and the reason your negotiated cloud discounts don't apply).
It reached GA in January 2026 after a Public Preview that opened in August 2025.

what.note: Compute access modes that determine Unity Catalog support, language availability, and whether a cluster is shared.

fact.note: The two original ways to attach compute to governed data: one isolated every user's Python, SQL and Scala so a crowd could safely share one cluster, the other gave a lone user privileged machine access for GPUs, R and RDD APIs.

limitations.note: On Standard access mode, Databricks Runtime for ML, GPU compute, R, RDD APIs, spark-submit tasks, Hive UDFs, the Scala SparkContext, and streaming socket sources aren't supported; compute runs as a low-privilege user with restricted filesystem access and can't reach the instance metadata service or other services in the Databricks VPC.

name: Standard and Dedicated access modes

what.note: Compute access modes that determine Unity Catalog support, language availability, and whether a cluster is shared.

fact.note: 'Shared' became 'Standard' and 'Single user' became 'Dedicated' in 2025 - then 'Dedicated' clusters were promptly allowed to be shared with a group, making the new name ironic on arrival.
A new 'Auto' mode now just picks the recommended access mode for you.

occasion.note: What's new with Unity Catalog compute, March 2025

prediction: Standard and Dedicated access modes v2

what.note: Long-lived bearer tokens used to authenticate to the Databricks REST API, CLI, and integrations.

fact.note: These long-lived bearer tokens authenticate to the REST API, CLI, and integrations - but they can't automate account-level functionality, and have a habit of ending up pasted into notebooks.
Databricks now says, where possible, to use OAuth instead 'because OAuth provides stronger security.'

limitations.note: Each service principal supports at most 20 federation policies (use separate service principals for separate workloads); the JWT must be signed with RS256 or ES256; and inline JWKS JSON allows at most 5 keys (use a JWKS URI for more).

what.note: Lets external systems authenticate to Databricks using their own identity provider's OIDC/JWT tokens, exchanged for Databricks access - with no stored Databricks secrets.

fact.note: Your own identity provider's OIDC tokens are exchanged for Databricks access on the fly, so there are no Databricks secrets to store, leak, or rotate at all.
OAuth token federation became generally available in August 2025, letting you reach Databricks APIs with tokens from your own identity provider instead of managing Databricks secrets.

links.label: Medium: OAuth token federation with Entra

what.note: The original Databricks SQL query editor UI.

fact.note: The original Databricks SQL query editor ran one statement per tab - and it's scheduled to retire entirely in late July 2026.
From late May 2026 the new editor became the default and the opt-out vanished - you can only switch back until the July retirement.

links.label: Databricks Blog: The New SQL Editor

what.note: The original Databricks SQL alerts that notified when a scheduled query crossed a threshold.

fact.note: These original SQL alerts fired when a scheduled query crossed a threshold - and each alert leaned on a separately saved query to do it.
Even its CLI command group is now marked deprecated.

links.label: Community: Legacy vs V2 SQL alerts

name: Init scripts on DBFS
replacement: Init scripts in Unity Catalog volumes or workspace files

what.note: Cluster initialization shell scripts stored on DBFS, plus the older legacy global and cluster-named init-script mechanisms.

fact.note: This deprecation has real teeth: leave your init scripts on DBFS and the cluster flatly refuses to start until you move them somewhere governed.
Legacy global init scripts were switched off for every workspace on September 1, 2023.

links.label: Community: Move init scripts off DBFS

name: No isolation shared access mode
replacement: Shared or Single user access mode

what.note: The legacy compute access mode that shares a cluster across users without process isolation or Unity Catalog governance.

fact.note: It shared one cluster across users with no process isolation between them and no Unity Catalog governance - so, unsurprisingly, new accounts don't get it by default anymore.
Admins can flip an account setting that disables credential sharing on these clusters.

links.label: Databricks Blog: Admin isolation on shared clusters

what.note: The original Databricks Connect client for running local code against a remote cluster, on Databricks Runtime 12.2 LTS and below.

fact.note: The original Databricks Connect ran local code against a remote cluster on Runtime 12.2 LTS and below, using its own bespoke client protocol that predated Spark Connect.

limitations.note: Clean rooms are limited to at most ten collaborators, can't be renamed after creation, and require serverless compute plus Unity Catalog with OpenSharing enabled.

what.note: A secure, privacy-protecting environment where multiple parties collaborate on sensitive data - sharing tables, volumes, and views and running approved notebook code - without direct access to each other's data.

fact.note: Two companies can run joint analysis on each other's data without either one seeing the other's raw rows - a 'no-trust' model where all parties get exactly equal privileges.
It reached GA on AWS and Azure in February 2025, with GCP following later that year.

occasion.note: GA on AWS and Azure, February 2025

what.note: The modern Databricks command-line interface - a standalone, dependency-free binary with complete coverage of the Databricks REST API.

fact.note: Databricks Asset Bundles aren't a separate tool - they're the `bundle` command group built into the CLI, so every `databricks bundle deploy` runs through it.
It carried 0.x version numbers for years and only crossed into its 1.0.0 GA release - its first major version - on May 21, 2026.

limitations.note: Doesn't support the display() API, Databricks Utilities (credentials, library, notebook workflow, widgets), SparkContext and RDDs, distributed ML training, or CREATE TABLE AS SELECT, with version-specific restrictions such as no streaming foreachBatch and no queries over 3600 seconds on Runtime 13.3 LTS and below.

what.note: The client library for running local DataFrame and Python code against a remote Databricks cluster, rebuilt on Spark Connect.

fact.note: It's built on Spark Connect's gRPC client/server split, so ordinary PySpark DataFrame code on your laptop runs on a remote cluster with no code changes between local and remote.
It requires Databricks Runtime 13.3 LTS or above.

limitations.note: Doesn't support bucketing, prohibits cross-type table cloning (managed-to-managed and external-to-external only), disallows workspace-level groups in GRANT statements, and imposes Databricks Runtime version requirements for Python UDFs, Scala UDFs, and R in dynamic views used for row- or column-level security.

what.note: The unified governance layer for data and AI across Databricks - a three-level namespace (catalog.schema.object) with centralized access control, lineage, and auditing.

fact.note: Its three-level namespace - catalog.schema.object - governs tables, models, functions, and files under one permission model, and it's the backbone nearly every modern Databricks feature now hangs off.

Announced at the Data and AI Summit 2021, it later reached general availability on AWS and Azure - bringing fine-grained auditing, data lineage down to the column and row level, and row- and column-level security through dynamic views.

limitations.note: Doesn't support stages, webhooks, email notifications, comment threads, or the activity log, and several search-API fields and operators (order_by, tag filters, and operators beyond exact equality) are unsupported.

what.note: Model lifecycle management governed by Unity Catalog - registered models live in the three-level namespace with aliases instead of stages.

fact.note: Registered models live in the catalog.schema namespace with aliases like @champion, so promoting a model to production is just repointing an alias - and every version carries Unity Catalog lineage and access control.
You migrate old model versions across with copy_model_version().

links.label: Medium: ML model lifecycle in UC

limitations.note: A FeatureSpec can contain at most 1000 features, at most 50 tables can train a model, and at most 100 on-demand features can be used in a model; Databricks Runtime ML clusters can't be used with Lakeflow pipelines (standard access mode clusters are required).

what.note: Feature management built on Unity Catalog - any Delta table with a primary key can serve as a feature table, with UC lineage and governance.

fact.note: A feature table is nothing more than a Delta table with a primary key constraint in Unity Catalog - updated, browsed, and governed through Unity Catalog like any other table.
Its Python package is databricks-feature-engineering, renamed from the older databricks-feature-store.

limitations.note: Can't be used for queries you don't own or that contain query-based dropdown lists, and shared query results are limited to 64000 rows.

what.note: The redesigned Databricks SQL query editor, with multi-statement tabs, inline results, and Genie-assisted authoring.

fact.note: It runs single or multi-statement queries and lets you chat with Genie Code to write, refine, or understand queries right in the editor.
It became the default editor for every workspace in late May 2026.

links.label: Databricks Blog: Introducing the New SQL Editor

limitations.note: An existing saved SQL query can't be reused when creating an alert (each alert owns its query definition), and alerts no longer support the legacy UNKNOWN status, resolving only to OK, TRIGGERED, or ERROR.

what.note: The rebuilt Databricks SQL alerts - threshold notifications on scheduled queries, created and managed from a single UI.

fact.note: It watches a scheduled query and fires when a threshold is crossed, with explicit OK / TRIGGERED / ERROR states and creation and management all in one UI.
The latest version of Databricks SQL alerts reached General Availability in May 2026, by which point Databricks reported more than 4,000 customers already using alerts in production.

what.note: A serverless way to build and deploy interactive data and AI applications - dashboards, chatbots, forms, ops tools - directly on Databricks, in common Python or JavaScript frameworks and governed by Unity Catalog.

fact.note: It takes an off-the-shelf Streamlit, Dash, Gradio, or React app and runs it on Databricks' own serverless compute with no infrastructure to provision - and the app is governed by Unity Catalog like everything else on the platform.
By its June 2025 GA, Databricks reported 20,000+ apps built by 2,500+ customers across 28 regions.

limitations.note: Up to 100 apps per workspace, with additional limits on Databricks Free Edition.

links.label: Databricks Blog: GA of Databricks Apps

what.note: A centralized governance and monitoring layer for model serving endpoints - permissions, rate limits, payload logging, and safety guardrails for the generative-AI models an organization uses.

fact.note: It bolts governance onto model serving without touching the model: flip it on and every request through an endpoint gets rate-limited, guardrailed, and logged to an inference table - and if an external model fails, it can fall back to another one on the same endpoint.
It entered public preview in September 2024 as "Mosaic AI Gateway," a centralized service that streamlines the usage and management of the generative-AI models an organization uses.

links.label: Databricks release notes: AI Gateway Public Preview (Sept 2024)
Databricks release notes: AI Gateway GA (June 2025)

what.note: GPU support for Databricks serverless compute - on-demand accelerators for deep-learning training and fine-tuning, single-node and multi-node, with no clusters to provision.

fact.note: It puts on-demand H100s behind serverless compute, so a deep-learning job spins up GPUs without anyone sizing a cluster - single-node tasks are in Public Preview while the distributed multi-GPU training API is still catching up in Beta.
It first showed up in June 2025 under the plainer name 'Serverless GPU compute.'

occasion.note: Debuted as Serverless GPU compute (Beta), Data + AI Summit June 2025

limitations.note: Only A10 and H100 accelerators; the maximum workload runtime is seven days; no FedRAMP High or DoD IL5 compliance. For scheduled jobs, Environments-panel dependencies and auto-recovery for incompatible packages aren't supported, and cross-region GPUs during peak demand can incur egress costs.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Serverless GPU compute Beta (June 2025)
Databricks release notes: AI Runtime Public Preview (March 2026)

what.note: Secrets stored and governed as securable objects in Unity Catalog, using the three-level namespace (catalog.schema.secret) and the same privileges and auditing as any other data asset.

fact.note: Your API keys finally get the same treatment as your tables: a secret becomes a first-class Unity Catalog securable at catalog.schema.secret, so the GRANTs and audit logs you already use for data now cover credentials - no more workspace-scoped secret scopes off to the side.
It entered Public Preview on July 10, 2026, replacing off-to-the-side workspace secret scopes with a first-class governed securable.

limitations.note: Not supported on SQL warehouses or in global/cluster init scripts; retrieval needs Databricks Runtime-backed notebooks and jobs. They don't appear in global search, BROWSE on a catalog doesn't apply, and there's no information schema yet; the API needs the unity-catalog OAuth scope. Capped at 100 secrets per schema and 1,000 per metastore.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Secrets in Unity Catalog Public Preview (July 2026)

what.note: A workspace add-on that unlocks managed disaster recovery plus the Enhanced Security and Compliance (ESC) controls for a single compute rate, enabled per workspace from the account console.

fact.note: It bundles managed disaster recovery with the Enhanced Security and Compliance controls into one per-workspace add-on billed at a single compute rate - and it's gated, so you turn it on by talking to your account team from a new Add-ons tab, not with a toggle.
It entered Public Preview on June 12, 2026.

limitations.note: Requires an Enterprise-plan workspace and the gated Managed DR preview; only account admins can toggle it, and it can't be turned off while a failover group is active. Enhanced security monitoring and automatic cluster update apply to classic compute only, and enabling the compliance security profile with standards permanently pins the workspace to CSP.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Mission Critical add-on Public Preview (June 2026)

what.note: An OpenSharing option that lets a provider share data from cloud storage behind a firewall or private endpoint without allowlisting each recipient's network.

fact.note: It lets a provider share data from storage behind a firewall or private endpoint without adding each collaborator's network to an allowlist, and it works for clean rooms too - Databricks brokers the connection through a managed proxy.
It debuted as 'OpenSharing SecureConnect' in June 2026.

limitations.note: Assets can't be backed by Cloudflare R2; not available on AWS GovCloud; and AWS PrivateLink to S3 isn't compatible with the FIPS endpoints Databricks uses by default in US regions. Recipient-side limits (such as mTLS support) also apply.

links.label: Databricks release notes: SecureConnect Public Preview (June 2026)

what.note: A centralized Unity Catalog interface for searching, browsing, and previewing governed data assets, organized into business domains.

fact.note: It's an internal data-marketplace front door to Unity Catalog: search, browse, and preview governed assets grouped into business domains, so consumers find data without spelunking the catalog tree.
It entered Beta on February 18, 2026.

limitations.note: Subdomains support only one level of nesting and must be created from a workspace-level Manage Discover page (no account-level subdomain management yet). Per-account domain and subdomain counts, and the number of tags you can apply, are capped (see Resource limits).

links.label: Databricks release notes: Discover page and domains Beta (February 2026)

what.note: Unity Catalog managed materialized views that are readable by external Apache Iceberg clients.

fact.note: It stretches Unity Catalog's managed Iceberg support from tables to materialized views, so an outside Iceberg reader like Snowflake or Trino can query a Databricks-maintained MV directly - though for now you enable it through your account team.
It entered Public Preview on July 7, 2026, and is gated behind your account team.

limitations.note: Public Preview is gated (enable via your Databricks account team). External Iceberg readers can read but not write the materialized view, and you must run REPAIR TABLE with SYNC METADATA before it's externally accessible.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Managed Iceberg materialized views Public Preview (July 2026)

what.note: An agent that converts legacy SQL dialects - T-SQL, Snowflake, Redshift, Oracle, BigQuery, Teradata - to ANSI SQL for migration projects, powered by Genie Code.

fact.note: Powered by Genie Code, it reads each legacy SQL script, checks the syntax and the semantic intent, and iteratively fixes its own errors before writing ANSI SQL back to your workspace - a migration grunt that debugs its own output.
It is part of the Lakebridge migration tooling and switches on from the workspace Previews page.

limitations.note: A batch can contain at most 300 files and a script at most 1,000 lines; Teradata BTEQ scripts are not yet supported.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Lakebridge Agentic Converter Beta (July 2026)

what.note: Automatically replays a sample of read-only serverless workloads against upcoming Databricks Runtime releases to catch regressions before they reach production.

fact.note: It quietly re-runs a sample of your read-only serverless workloads against the next Databricks Runtime before you upgrade, so a runtime regression shows up in a shadow run instead of in prod - no setup, your data never leaves the workspace, and the replay compute isn't billed.
It is in Beta, enabled by a workspace admin from the Previews page.

limitations.note: Replays only read-only SQL and DataFrame workloads on serverless SQL warehouses, notebooks, and jobs, reading Unity Catalog Delta tables. Writes, UDFs, AI functions like ai_query, federated queries, and ABAC/RBAC workloads are excluded; for DataFrames only the submitted Spark plan is replayed, not the preceding Python cells.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Lakehouse Replay Beta (June 2026)

what.note: Declarative pipelines - materialized views and streaming tables - created from a notebook on serverless general compute, in SQL or Python.

fact.note: Renamed from "Pipelines for Databricks SQL," they let you build materialized views and streaming tables straight from a notebook on serverless general compute, in SQL or Python, with no dedicated pipeline cluster to stand up.
They were previously called "Pipelines for Databricks SQL" - renamed once they could also run from a notebook on serverless general compute, not just a SQL warehouse.

limitations.note: On serverless general compute: only the table owner can refresh; synchronous refresh only (no async); current channel only (no preview); a table refreshes only with the compute type it was created with; and cost attribution, vertical autoscaling on out-of-memory errors, schema-upgrade retries, and performance-mode selection aren't available.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Standalone pipelines Beta (May 2026)

what.note: APIs to declaratively define and compute time-windowed aggregation features in Unity Catalog, materialize them to Delta or Lakebase, and serve them to online model serving.

fact.note: You declare a time-windowed aggregation once and it computes the feature for you - materializing to Delta or Lakebase and wiring into online model serving - the feature-engineering echo of the declarative-pipelines idea.
It launched in Beta in March 2026, initially only in the AWS us-east-1 and us-west-2 regions.

limitations.note: Beta, initially available only in the AWS us-east-1 and us-west-2 regions; features are defined in Unity Catalog.

links.label: Databricks release notes: Declarative Feature Engineering APIs Beta (March 2026)
